entry.title: P7995 [USACO21DEC] Walking Home B Solution

entry.content:
The content below was generated entirely by machine translation. Please verify its accuracy. If anything is unclear, consult the Chinese source version.

1: Understanding the Problem

Given an N×NN\times NN×N region containing two types of points, J and B, add one J point (or do not add one; when adding, it cannot be placed where a B point already exists), and find the largest square consisting of J points.

2: Analysis

2.1: Summary

Because the data is small (N<100)(N<100)(N<100), and one J edge determines the other two edges of a square, we can enumerate J edges and check them to find the largest square.

2.2: A Little Mathematics

Suppose points P1P_1P1​ and P2P_2P2​ determine a line, and the vertical coordinate of P1P_1P1​ is always higher than that of P2P_2P2​. We can draw the following figure:

How do we calculate the coordinates of P3P_3P3​ and P4P_4P4​?

By observation, the four triangles in the figure are congruent. We only need to calculate the lengths of the long and short legs of the triangle (or the two different perpendicular legs; in this special case, the long and short legs are arranged as in the figure), then add an offset to obtain the coordinates of P3P_3P3​ and P4P_4P4​.

Long leg of the triangle:

L△=b1−b2L_{\triangle}= b_1 - b_2L△​=b1​−b2​

Short leg of the triangle:

S△=a1−a2S_{\triangle}= a_1 - a_2S△​=a1​−a2​

From these two formulas, we obtain the coordinates:

P3=(a2+L△,b2−S△)P_3 = (a_2 + L_{\triangle}, b_2 - S_{\triangle})P3​=(a2​+L△​,b2​−S△​)

P4=(a1+L△,b1−S△)P_4 = (a_1 + L_{\triangle}, b_1 - S_{\triangle})P4​=(a1​+L△​,b1​−S△​)

In addition to the arrangement shown, the line P1P2P_1P_2P1​P2​ determines another square:

Again, we can obtain the coordinates of P5P_5P5​ and P6P_6P6​ using the preceding method. For P3P_3P3​ and P4P_4P4​, we added or subtracted the two different triangle-leg lengths (L△(L_{\triangle}(L△​ and S△)S_{\triangle})S△​) from an offset. By observation, applying the opposite operations to the offset gives P5P_5P5​ and P6P_6P6​:

P5=(a2−L△,b2+S△)P_5 = (a_2 - L_{\triangle}, b_2 + S_{\triangle})P5​=(a2​−L△​,b2​+S△​)

P6=(a1−L△,b1+S△)P_6 = (a_1 - L_{\triangle}, b_1 + S_{\triangle})P6​=(a1​−L△​,b1​+S△​)

Because we need the area of the square, calculate it as:

area=(a1−a2)2+(b1−b2)2\text{area} = (a_1 - a_2)^2 + (b_1 - b_2)^2area=(a1​−a2​)2+(b1​−b2​)2

2.3: Program Idea

We can now obtain the coordinates of P3∼6P_{3\sim6}P3∼6​. Next, determine whether the two squares determined by one edge are valid.

Because we may freely place one J point, a square may contain only three existing J points. The additional J point must be placed at an unoccupied point.

Therefore, the square made of P1∼4P_{1\sim4}P1∼4​ is valid when:

(P3∈JANDP4∉B) OR (P3∉BANDP4∈J)(P_3 \in \text{J} \quad \text{AND}\quad P_4 \notin \text{B})\ \text{OR}\ (P_3 \notin \text{B} \quad\text{AND}\quad P_4 \in \text{J})(P3​∈JANDP4​∈/B) OR (P3​∈/BANDP4​∈J)

Note: J denotes the set of J points, and B denotes the set of B points.

The square made of P1,2,5,6P_{1,2,5,6}P1,2,5,6​ is handled similarly.

3: Implementation and Improvements

3.1: First Attempt

After thinking of the approach, I quickly wrote the code, but two test points timed out.

Submission

3.2: Second Attempt

The code performs a great deal of useless calculation. We assumed that P1P_1P1​’s vertical coordinate is always higher than P2P_2P2​’s, so the program contains a check to ensure that assumption:

We enumerate all J-point pairs with two loops, so each edge can be enumerated twice in reverse order. We can skip such cases directly:

After this, every point entering the square-validity checks satisfies our assumption, and duplicate computation is reduced.

Further analysis shows that the loop only checks square validity. If the area of a square found during enumeration is no larger than the current answer, there is no need to check it, so skip it directly:

After this improvement, the solution is accepted:

Accepted submission

entry.title: P2867 [USACO06NOV] Big Square S Solution

entry.content:
The content below was generated entirely by machine translation. Please verify its accuracy. If anything is unclear, consult the Chinese source version.

Problem link

The reading experience is better on the blog

Preface: This solution may be verbose because I wrote down my entire thought process. If you already have the basic idea or want a concise solution, you can skip it.

1: Understanding the Problem

There are cowcowcow cows and typetypetype adjective classes. Class iii has numinum_inumi​ adjectives, and the jjjth adjective in class iii is adji,jadj_{i,j}adji,j​. Every cow is described by one adjective from each class in order. We are told to remove nnn of the cowcowcow cows. Among the remaining cow−ncow-ncow−n cows sorted lexicographically, which cow is in position kkk?

2: Analysis and Reformulation

2.1: Connection with Number Systems

In the sample, n=3n=3n=3 and k=7k=7k=7, with the following adjectives:

adji,jadj_{i,j}adji,j​j=1j=1j=1j=2j=2j=2j=3j=3j=3
i=1i=1i=1 (first class)largesmallN/A
i=2i=2i=2 (second class)brownwhitespotted
i=3i=3i=3 (third class)noisysilentN/A

Lexicographic comparison gives earlier positions greater influence: compare the first character first, then the second, and finally the third. Thus the first adjective class has the greatest influence, followed by the second, then the third.

This is closely related to a positional number system. In (123456789)10(123456789)_{10}(123456789)10​, digit 1 represents 100000000100000000100000000, digit 2 represents 200000002000000020000000, and a digit xxx at position iii represents 10i−1x10^{i-1}x10i−1x. In base kkk, it represents ki−1xk^{i-1}xki−1x.

2.2: Solving the Simplified Problem

First assume every adjective class contains exactly kkk adjectives. Sort every class lexicographically and store the ranks in rank[i][j]. This maps each adjective to a digit in a base-kkk number system. The smaller the adjective class index, the greater its lexicographic influence, so it corresponds to a higher digit position.

After mapping adjectives to digits, convert a decimal rank to base kkk, then map the digits back to adjectives.

For example, suppose the three classes are {a,b}, {c,d}, and {e,f}. With ranks starting at 0, the rank table is:

ranki,jrank_{i,j}ranki,j​j=0j=0j=0j=1j=1j=1
i=1i=1i=1ab
i=2i=2i=2cd
i=3i=3i=3ef

To find the cow at lexicographic position 3, convert 3 to binary, (011)2(011)_2(011)2​, reverse it to (110)2 reversed(110)_{2\text{ reversed}}(110)2 reversed​, and map the digits back to classes. The answer is a, d, f.

2.3: The Original Problem

In the original problem, the number of adjectives differs by class, so each digit position has a different base. In the sample, the bases from the least significant class upward are 2,3,22,3,22,3,2.

To convert a decimal rank, precompute the decimal value represented by each position. The first position has weight 1; each preceding position’s weight is multiplied by the number of values in that position. Then repeatedly select the digit for each position:

Finally, the requested rank is among the remaining cows rather than all cows. Compute the ranks of the nnn removed cows. For every removed cow whose rank is less than or equal to kkk, increment kkk; then convert the resulting rank back to adjectives.

Code and Details

That is the end of the solution. If you find a problem or something unclear, feel free to contact me privately or leave a comment.

entry.title: P3087 [USACO13NOV] Farmer John Has No Large Brown Cow S Solution

entry.content:
The content below was generated entirely by machine translation. Please verify its accuracy. If anything is unclear, consult the Chinese source version.

I saw that the existing solutions did not use an STL vector, so I came to submit one.

Problem link

1: Reformulating the Problem

There are ppp nodes and ccc undirected edges in a graph, and there are nnn nodes that cannot be deleted. Find the minimum number of nodes that need to be deleted so that none of these nnn fixed points can reach node 1.

2: Analysis and Modeling

After reformulating the problem, we see that we need to delete some nodes (as few as possible) to make the entire graph become two disconnected parts. The minimum-cut (maximum-flow) algorithm in network-flow algorithms can handle this problem.

【Students unfamiliar with maximum-flow algorithms can first solve this template problem】
Maximum-flow template

However, ordinary minimum cut handles “deleting some edges of a graph so that its two parts become disconnected,” while this problem asks us to delete some nodes. Therefore, we need to convert nodes into edges.

I use the method of splitting every node into two nodes (an outgoing node and an incoming node). For a specific implementation, refer to the solution for P1345 Telecowmunication.

Here is a brief explanation. First, split every point in the graph into two points: an outgoing point and an incoming point.

There is a directed edge connecting these two nodes:

Every directed edge pointing to this point can only connect to the point’s incoming node. Every directed edge starting from this point can only start at its outgoing node.

What is the use of splitting every node into an outgoing point and an incoming point? In an ordinary minimum-cut problem, if we want to know how many edges must be removed to disconnect the sink and source of a graph, we can set the weight of every edge to 111, paying a cost of 111 to delete that edge.

In a minimum-cut problem based on cut vertices, we can set the weight of the edge connecting each node’s outgoing and incoming points to 111. Then, if we want to delete this node, we can pay a cost of 111 to cut this edge, which deletes the node as well.

This raises a problem: the statement explicitly says that some nodes cannot be deleted. If all weights are set to 111, how do we handle nodes that cannot be deleted?

For these key nodes (the undeletable nodes), we can set the capacity of their internal edges to INFINFINF, so the algorithm will not delete them (a minimum-cut algorithm computes the minimum cost that makes the graph disconnected, and setting a capacity to INFINFINF makes deleting that node very uneconomical).

Also note that, in addition to the key points mentioned in the statement, the source and sink cannot be deleted either, so this needs to be handled when building the graph. The problem asks for the minimum number of deleted nodes, so the edges connecting these nodes cannot be deleted either; their capacities must be set to INFINFINF.

After resolving the edge-capacity issue, we consider the source and sink. We can set node 1 as the source and connect every key point to the sink. The resulting answer is the minimum number of nodes to delete so that none of the key points can reach node 1 (if any key point can reach node 1, then the sink can also reach node 1).

Summary of the Graph-Building Steps

  1. Split every node into an incoming node and an outgoing node, with an internal edge between them.
  2. For a deletable node, set the capacity of its internal edge to 111.
  3. For an undeletable node, set the capacity of its internal edge to INFINFINF.
  4. The undeletable edges include:
    1. The source’s internal edge.
    2. The sink’s internal edge.
    3. The edges connecting every node.
    4. The internal edges of key points.
  5. Set the source to node 1 and connect the sink to every key point.

3: Algorithm

I use the Dinic algorithm, because each augmentation can find multiple augmenting paths, making it faster than the EK algorithm. If you are unfamiliar with it, see the solution for the maximum-flow template problem mentioned above.

4: Implementation Details

When implementing node splitting, we can set the number of a node’s incoming point to the node’s own number, and set the number of its outgoing point to its own number plus ppp (the total number of nodes). This ensures that there are no duplicate numbers.

When implementing the Dinic algorithm, we need to operate on reverse edges. I use an STL vector to store edges, so I add a rev (reverse) variable to the node structure to record the index of the current edge’s reverse edge.

This is my first time writing a solution, so there may be many problems. If you find anything incorrect, feel free to point it out in the comments or contact me privately. Questions about anything unclear are also welcome. Finally, if this solution helped you, please give it a like or share your thoughts in the comments.
